name: Upper Volta (1959-1984)
description: Three equal horizontal stripes — black, white, and red from top to bottom.
history: Adopted on 9 December 1959, shortly before Upper Volta gained full independence from France on 5 August 1960. The black-white-red tricolour represented the three Volta tributaries that flowed through the country and gave it its colonial-era French name (Haute-Volta). The flag was used by the Republic of Upper Volta and continued through the Sahelian droughts of the 1970s and the 1966 and 1980 coups d'etat. It was replaced on 4 August 1984 when revolutionary leader Thomas Sankara renamed the country Burkina Faso ('Land of the Upright People') and adopted the present red-and-green flag.

features.symbolism: the colours of the three principal rivers of Upper Volta — the Black Volta, the White Volta and the Red Volta — which gave the country its name
